specification.topic-statement: The tab completion system maintains a filtered list of command and argument candidates, updating the input buffer with the selected candidate when the user presses Tab and dismissing when they accept, revert, or navigate away.

scope.in-scope: Candidate generation for commands and arguments, fuzzy filtering, cycling and navigation, input buffer replacement, revert-on-escape behavior, and completion panel visibility.
scope.boundary: Completion requests the full list of registered commands to build command candidates. The command names and descriptions are consumed as-is; the registry internals are out of scope.
Completion reads the current buffer text and replaces it wholesale when a candidate is selected. The buffer's cursor management and layout internals are out of scope.
Candidate filtering uses a fuzzy scoring algorithm. This specification documents the observable filtering behavior — the scoring algorithm internals are out of scope.

field.name: candidates
field.note: filtered and scored; at most 15 entries

field.name: current index
field.note: zero-based index into candidates; wraps on navigation

field.name: prefix
field.note: the input text that triggered this completion

field.name: original input
field.note: the full input buffer text at the moment Tab was first pressed; used to revert on Escape

behavior.activation:
The completion context is created and kept active when the user types characters into an input buffer that begins with `/`. After each character insertion or deletion, the current buffer text is used as the new prefix to rebuild the candidate list. If the buffer no longer starts with `/`, the completion context is dismissed without reverting the input.
If no candidates match the current prefix, the completion context is not created or is dismissed if previously active.
The completion context is created and maintained by character input, not by the Tab key. When Tab is pressed, it operates on the already-active context. The Tab key creates a context on its own only when one is not already active at the moment of the keypress.
behavior.candidate-generation:
Two categories of completion are produced depending on whether the input contains a space after the command name.
When the input contains only a command prefix (no space), candidates are drawn from the full list of registered commands. The text after the leading `/` is used as the search term against command names.
Candidates are filtered to those where all characters of the search term appear in order within the command name (case-insensitively). Among those, only candidates where the search term forms a case-insensitive prefix are retained. The retained candidates are scored and returned in descending score order, with at most 15 candidates.
When the input contains a space after the command name, argument completion applies. The text after the space is used as the search term. The available candidates depend on the command:
For the `/model` command: if the search term contains a `/`, candidates are the four thinking levels: `min`, `low`, `med`, and `high`. Otherwise, candidates are drawn from a fixed list of known model names.
For the `/debug` command: candidates are `off` and `on`.
For the `/rewind` command: candidates are the labels of all marks currently set on the active agent, in the order they appear.
For all other commands: no argument completion is provided and no completion context is created.
The same fuzzy prefix filter and 15-candidate limit apply to argument candidates as to command candidates.
behavior.tab-key-behavior:
Pressing Tab advances the selected candidate and replaces the input buffer content with the new selection. The completion context is dismissed after the input is updated.
When a completion context is active at the time of the Tab keypress, the current index is incremented by one, wrapping back to zero when it would exceed the last candidate. The input buffer is replaced with the text corresponding to the new selection (see input replacement below). The completion context is then dismissed.
When no completion context is active at the time of the Tab keypress, one is created for the current buffer text using the same candidate generation rules. The current index is set to zero. The input buffer is replaced with the text corresponding to the first candidate. The completion context is then dismissed.
If the buffer is empty or does not begin with `/`, the Tab keypress has no effect.
If no candidates match the current buffer text, the Tab keypress has no effect.
Each Tab press completes one selection and dismisses the context. If Tab is pressed again, a new context is created from the now-updated buffer text. Because the buffer was just updated to contain a full command or argument name, the next context's candidates will be filtered against that complete name.
behavior.input-replacement:
When a candidate is selected, the input buffer is replaced wholesale with the constructed completion text. The original prefix is preserved up to the point where the candidate replaces it.
For command completion, the replacement is the `/` character followed by the selected candidate name.
For argument completion, the replacement is everything from the beginning of the input up to and including the first space, followed by the selected candidate. The command portion of the input is preserved unchanged.
When a Space key is pressed while a context is active, the selected candidate is committed with a trailing space appended to the replacement text. The context is dismissed.
behavior.escape-behavior:
When Escape is pressed while a completion context is active and the original input was recorded at activation time, the input buffer is replaced with the original input text, reverting any candidate selections. The completion context is then dismissed.
If no original input was recorded when Escape is pressed, the completion context is dismissed without reverting the input buffer.
behavior.navigation-dismissal: Arrow key presses (left or right) dismiss the active completion context without reverting the input buffer. The buffer retains whatever text was most recently inserted by a candidate selection.
behavior.panel-visibility:
A completion panel is rendered while a completion context is active. The panel occupies one row per candidate. Each row displays the candidate name alongside its command description (for command completion). The row corresponding to the current index is rendered with reverse video and bold formatting. All other rows are rendered without additional formatting. Each row is padded to the full terminal width.
The panel is hidden when the completion context is inactive (dismissed).
behavior.index-navigation:
The current index advances by one on each Tab press, wrapping from the last candidate (index `count − 1`) back to the first (index 0).
A backward navigation operation (returning to the previous candidate) is defined in the implementation but is not connected to any key press.
The implementation includes logic to move the current index to the previous candidate with wrap-around, but no keyboard shortcut or other input event triggers this operation. It is unreachable during normal use.
